FPPC
JUN 20 11:27 AM '90

TOWN of LOS GATOS
Office of the Town Attorney
(408) 354-6880


Federal Express


June 19, 1990


Ms. Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re:   Request for Advice on behalf of Councilmembers
      Thomas J. Ferrito, Brent N. Ventura and Joanne
      Benjamin.

Dear Ms. Donovan:

I have been authorized by Thomas J. Ferrito, Brent N. Ventura and Joanne
Benjamin, Councilmembers of the Town of Los Gatos, to seek on their behalf this
request for formal written advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).
Councilmember Thomas J. Ferrito's mailing address is 9 Simons Way, Los Gatos,
CA 95030. Councilmember Brent Ventura's mailing address is 131 Broadway, Los
Gatos, CA 95030. Councilmember Joanne Benjamin's mailing address is 109
Worcester, Los Gatos, CA 95030.

This request seeks guidance on the obligations under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1976 for Councilmembers Ferrito, Ventura
and Benjamin. More specifically, we request your opinion regarding whether the
acceptance of travel and subsistence expenses provided by the Jewish Federation of
Greater San Jose (Federation) in connection with the annual Conference of Mayors
held in Israel requires disqualification from participation in a land use decision
which would have a material financial effect on the Jewish Community Center
(JCC). The difficulty in the analysis of this situation stems from the fact that the


CIVIC CENTER • 110 EAST MAIN STREET • P.O. BOX 949 • LOS GATOS, CALIFORNIA 95031

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
June 19, 1990
Page 2


Federation has a significant relationship with the JCC, such that the JCC may appear
to be an "instrumentality" of the Federation.

STATEMENT OF FACTS

The Federation is a nonprofit, tax-exempt charitable organization. It provided air
fare and reasonable subsistence to three councilmembers to allow them to attend
the annual Conference of Mayors held in Israel in April of 1989 and 1990. The trip
was valued at $2100 and was determined by this office, in consultation with the staff
of the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC), to be a gift.

The JCC applied to the Town for an architectural and site permit and a conditional
use permit to expand its facilities by constructing a health and fitness center which
has been valued at $3,051,568. (See the attached memorandum from Scott Baker,
Director of Building Services.) The permits were denied by the Planning
Commission and the JCC is now appealing to the Town Council. The hearing on
the appeal was continued from June 18, 1990 to July 16, 1990.

Although the Federation and the JCC are incorporated separately, hold autonomous
meetings of the Board of Directors and have independent operating policies, they
also share four (4) common board members. Additionally, the Federation is housed
at the JCC facility where it pays .75 cents per square foot a month (a rent which is
considered below market) and of a $1.5 million operating budget, the Federation
provides $140,000 worth of funding to the JCC annually. The yearly operating
budget for the JCC is $1,292,000; the money received from the Federation represents
9 percent of that budget.

Enclosed you will find my agenda report to Council, which includes an excerpted
confidential opinion on the subject. It is respectfully requested that you expedite the
issuance of your advice letter since the Town Council is scheduled to address this
matter on July 16, 1990.

TOWN EVALUATION

The analysis of the applicable statutes and regulations concludes that there is, at a
minimum, the appearance of a conflict of interest concerning the councilmembers'
participation on the JCC appeal.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
June 19, 1990
Page 3


Acceptance of the trip to Israel was not a violation of Proposition 73 (Government
Code Section 85400) which prohibits elected officials from accepting from a single
source a gift or gifts aggregating $1,000 or more in any calendar year because the
money provided by the Federation was solely reimbursement for actual
transportation expenses and subsistence in connection with chairing and participating
in panel discussions on governmental processes.

To avoid the burden of proving that the councilmembers provided consideration of
equal or greater value for the cost of the trip ($2,100) pursuant to Government Code
82028, I recommended to the Councilmembers that the trip be considered a gift and
not income.

Government Code Section 87103(e) provides that an official who receives gifts
aggregating $250 or more must disqualify himself or herself from participating in any
decision which would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
source of the gifts. FPPC Regulation 18702.5 provides guidance on determining
whether the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision on a nonprofit organization
which has made gifts will be considered material.

In reviewing how FPPC Regulation 18702.5(d)(2) applied to the Federation and the
JCC, although it was unclear whether or not the health and fitness center would
effect the allocation of funds from the Federation to the JCC, it was clear that the
JCC's assets would increase by over $100,000 dollars since the new building was
valued at $3,051,568.

Furthermore, it is possible the Federation would either incur or avoid additional
expenses or reduce or eliminate existing expenses in the amount of $25,000 by virtue
of its connections with the JCC. Neither the Federation or the JCC have provided
this office with any information that the Federation would not be effected by the
construction of the health and fitness building.

Based on this analysis, I have given conservative advice that the three
councilmembers consider disqualifying themselves, and that the "legally required
participation" process provided by Regulation 18701 be followed. The Council has
requested an advice letter from the FPPC to clarify the councilmembers'
participation.

Again, I would sincerely appreciate your prompt attention to this matter. If you
have further questions, please feel free to contact me. Further information about

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
June 19, 1990
Page 4


the organizations involved may be obtained from Michael Papo, Executive Director,
Jewish Federation of Greater San Jose at (408) 358-3033. You may also wish to
reference FPPC advice letter to Mr. Papo A-39-636 (December 12, 1989).

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Mary J. Levinger
Town Attorney

cc:   Mayor and Town Council
      Robert F. Beyer, Interim Town Manager
      Michael Papo, Executive Director, Federation of Greater San Jose


Attachments:    Town of Los Gatos Council Agenda Report for
                June 18, 1990 Council

                Memorandum Dated January 16, 1990 RE: Valuation of Gym
                at Jewish Community Center

JUL 9 8 48 AM '90

ADDISON-PENZAK
Jewish Community Center
OF GREATER SAN JOSE

14855 Oka Road
Los Gatos, California 95030
(408) 358-3636

July 6, 1990

Robert Hudson

Stephen Brenner

Edward Wilhelm
Recording President

Joshua B. Malks, ACSW
Executive Director

Board of Trustees
Bernie Alter
Ken Bloomfield
Doris Davis
Dr. Barbara Dubins
Barry Frank
Rabbi Joseph Gitin
Charles Jacobds
Sybil Leonard
Jeanette Lowon
Rhoda Lynch
William Mancini
Sylvia Metz
Sandra Paller
Evelyn Perl
Marilyn Pepper
Dan Pulcrano
Neil Reinhard
Donald Sherman
Fran Shulman
Jil Silberstein
Herb Terrell
Dr. Toby Weisner
Cecil Weintraub

Past Presidents
Cockie Addison
Dr. William Starr
Leonard Meltz
Dr. Joseph Decker
Arnold Adelson
Harry Folberg
Maureen Starr
Henrietta Epstein
Joseph Riggio
Barbara Weiss

Gerda Opper
Honorary Member

An agency of the
Jewish Federation of Greater San Jose
and United Way of Santa Clara County

A member agency of JWB

Ms. Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel, Legal Division
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Donovan:

I am writing in connection with a letter to Ms.
Kathryn E. Donovan dated June 19, 1990 from Mary Jo
Levinger, Town Attorney for the Town of Los Gatos.

My interest in this matter is that I am the
President of the Jewish Community Center (JCC) of
Greater San Jose that is located in Los Gatos,
California. The Center is seeking approval from
the Town of Los Gatos for a building and use permit
to allow us to construct and use a gym and general
exercise facility. The Town Attorney has advised
three members of the Town Council, Mr. Thomas J.
Ferrito, Mr. Brent N. Ventura, and Ms. Joanne
Benjamin, of the possibility of the appearance of a
conflict of interest inasmuch as these council
members attended an International Conference of
Mayors in Jerusalem, Israel as guests of the Jewish
Federation of Greater San Jose. These three
council members have asked the City Attorney to
seek guidance from the Fair Political Practices
Commission with reference to this matter. The Town
Attorney's opinion states, in part that:

"while there may be no legal conflict
of interest, given the facts and the
circumstances surrounding the vote, the
appearance of a conflict of interest
certainly presents itself...." The law
clerk, Ms. Jennifer Hagen who prepared
the opinion, then concludes with
reference to these councilmembers,
"therefore, I believe it would be most
prudent to act conservatively on this
matter, and as a unit, excuse
themselves from the vote."

It is my belief, and those of the other members
of the Community Center, that there is no conflict of
interest, apparent or otherwise.

-1-

In the body of the opinion -- from Section 87103(e) -- an
official who receives gifts aggregating $250 or more must
disqualify himself or herself from participating in any decision
which would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
affect, distinguishable from its affect on the public generally,
on the source of the gifts (italics added). Since it is clear
that the decision will have a material financial affect on the
Community Center, the question is:

Is the Community Center the source of the gifts?

The source of the gifts was and is the Jewish Federation
exclusively. All of the funds for such trip expenses were provided
solely by the Federation. No portion of the funds came, directly
or indirectly, from the Community Center. In fact, the Community
Center was not advised of the advance of the funds, did not
recommend the advance of the funds, did not direct the advance of
the funds, and in fact, had no knowledge of the payment of such
funds or the making of such trips until recently, long after the
trips were made. There is not a shadow of substance to the
allegation that the Jewish Community Center is or was the source
of any portion of the funds provided for the trips to the
conference.

If the Federation is the source of the funds, will the
decision have a reasonably foreseeable affect on it?

No. The decision will have no affect on the Federation.
There will be no benefit or burden to the Federation of any kind
as a consequence of the decision to be made upon the application
of the Community Center.

Section 18702.5(d)(1) provides, in part as follows: "The
affect of a decision is material as to a non-profit entity which
is a source of income or gifts to the official if any of the
following applies: (d) for an entity whose gross annual receipts
are more than $1 million, but less than or equal to $10 million
the affect of the decision will be any of the following: (1) the
decision will result in an increase or decrease of the entity's
gross annual receipts for fiscal year in the amount of $100,000 or
more."

There will be no change in the gross annual receipts of the
Jewish Federation in this fiscal year or any other as a
consequence of the decision. No portion of the fees or revenues
of funds from the Jewish Community Center's general operating
budget, or those referrable to the operation of the exercise
facility contemplated, would enure to the benefit, directly or
indirectly of the Federation. All such funds are the exclusive
property of and under the exclusive domain and control of the
Community Center.

(2) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid
additional expense or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for
a fiscal year in the amount of $25,000 or more.

-2-

The expenses of the Jewish Federation will not be affected in
any way by the development, construction, ownership, operation or
financing of the facilities. No expenses will be reduced; no
additional expenses will be incurred. The Community Center is the
sole owner and operator of the property and the facility and the
sole executor of its programs. All of the income or receipts
received in connection with the facility, its construction or
operation insure to the benefit of the Community Center; all of the
expenses incurred in connection with the facility, its construction
or operation will be the burden of the Community Center.

(3) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of
$100,000 or more.

There will be no change of any kind in the value of the
Jewish Federation's assets or liabilities as a consequence of the
construction or operation of the Community Center's exercise
facility. The Federation is solely a tenant under a lease. All
of its rights to the use and benefit of the premises, either
existing or to be constructed, are spelled out in that document.
The only rights to the premises enjoyed by the Federation are the
rights to occupy a portion of the Community Center's premises as a
tenant and to use the library one (1) week each month. They have
no other rights. I have attached a copy of that document for your
inspection. No portion of any change, whether increase or
decrease, in the value of the Community Center's assets will be
redound to the benefit of the Federation either directly or
indirectly.

On the other hand, it is entirely likely that the Community
Center's gross annual receipts would increase in the amount of
$100,000 or more; it is equally clear that the decision would
cause the Community Center to incur additional expenses in the
amount of $25,000 or more, and we certainly do not dispute that
the decision would result in an increase in the value of the
Community Center's assets in the amount of $100,000 or more. But
since it is the position that was the source of the gift and not
the Community Center, these results are both apparently and
substantively legal and proper.

Nonetheless, is the Community Center an instrumentality of
the Federation so that in fact the prohibited changes in revenues,
expenses or assets really enures to the benefit of the Federation?

At page one in the letter of transmittal of June 1990 to your
office: "The difficulty in the analysis of the situation stems
from the fact that the Federation has a significant relationship
with the JCC, such that the JCC may appear to be an
"instrumentality" of the Federation."

At page two in the Council Agenda Report of June 15, 1990:
..."and that the JCC, arguably is an "instrumentality" of the
Federation...."

and

-3-

At page one of the Confidential Opinion from Jennifer Hagan,
Law Clerk to Mary Jo Leidinger, Town Attorney, dated February 1,
1990 "although the two organizations are incorporated separately,
hold autonomous meetings of the Board of Directors and have
independent operating policies, there is such a relationship
between them at (sic) there is the appearance that the Federation
and the Jewish Community Center are not distinct corporate
structures, but that the Jewish Community Center is an
"instrumentality" of the Federation."...

Neither the confidential opinion of the clerk, nor the
opinion of town council in the council agenda report, nor the
letter of transmittal prepared by town council on behalf of the
three councilmembers defines "instrumentality."

The entire opinion turns on the definition of the word and we
are left to guess as to its meaning. Categorically, the JCC
is not the agent of the Federation, is not its servant, it is not its
subsidiary, nor is it its alter ego. The Center's actions and
policies are not directed or controlled in any way by the
Federation. The Jewish Community Center is not the means by which
the Federation operates.

The law clerk is led from the "appearance" that the Jewish
Community Center is an instrumentality of the Federation to the
conclusion there is the "appearance" of a Conflict of Interest.
This conclusion can only be drawn if the Jewish Community Center
as such "instrumentality" is the real source of the funds for the
gift. This is false.

The letter from the Town Attorney of June 19, 1990, copy
which is attached, does not set forth a complete statement of the
facts in the matter.

From the opinion, the conclusion is bottomed on four items,
as follows:

1.    The fact that "the fact that the Federation is housed at
      the Jewish Community Center facilities where it occupies
      1,920 square feet of space. This office space is leased
      on a five (5) year contract where the Federation pays
      $17,000 annually, or $.75 per square foot a month. Lee
      Bowman indicates this figure reflects a monthly rent below
      fair market, at this time." (italics added)

The facilities of the Jewish Community Center consist of the
premises formerly known as the Berry Street Elementary School. The
grounds are approximately 8.783 acres; the school structure itself
contains approximately 35,000 square feet of habitable space. The
Jewish Community Center purchased this site as California surplus
school property in 1984. At the time of its purchase, the school had
been used as the headquarters for the state sponsored Medfiy
eradication program throughout several counties including Santa Clara
County. After such use, the premises were essentially abandoned. At
the time that the Community Center purchased the property and

-4-

presented a portion of it to the Federation for lease, use and
occupancy, it was thoroughly dilapidated and uninhabitable. The
Jewish Federation was our first tenant and the Federation had to pay
over $50,000 of its own funds to make the two classrooms rented from
us into useable office space. The Community Center and the
Federation signed a five (5) year lease commencing on October 5, 1985
and when the amounts that the Federation was required to spend on its
own behalf are amortized over the life of the lease, it reflects an
additional $.44 plus per square foot added to the beginning rental
rate of $.65 or $1.09 per square foot in the beginning year. As an
experienced real estate developer of many years, the rental of the
premises in such condition at that time for an effective $1.00 per
square foot was a full and fair charge. At the present time, the
base rent is $.44 per square foot pursuant to lease structured
increases for an effective of $1.19 per square foot. Other than the
free use of the Community Center Library one time each month, no
other Community Center facility is a part of their tenancy. I have
attached a copy of the Lease itself to my letter for your further
guidance.

The Jewish Community Center also leases space to other tenants
besides the Federation. It leases approximately 6,775 square feet to
the religious elementary school. That lease, as well as the
Federation, was entered into in 1985. The beginning rental was $.72
per square feet, on a 10 month basis, and has since raised to $.86
per square feet, also on a 10 month basis. It is a full service
lease as well. The Community Center pays for all utilities, cleaning
and maintenance.

It leases approximately 1,910 square feet to the Jewish Family
Service. This lease was entered in 1986 at $.72 per square feet.
The rental has since raised to $.81 per square feet on a full year
basis at present.

Rather than paying less than the other tenants, the Federation
pays more.

2.     "Additionally, the Federation and the Jewish Community
       Center share four (4) common board members,..."

There are 59 directors in total for the Jewish Federation and
the Jewish Community Center. The Jewish Federation has a total of 36
directors. The Community Center has a total of 23 directors. No
member of the Community Center Board of Directors serves as an
officer of the Federation. The only officer of the community center
that serves as a member of the Federation Board of Directors is
myself in my capacity as the President of the Community Center.
Other than myself, no member of the Federation Board of Directors
serves as an officer of the Community Center.

3.     "And of a $1.5 million operating budget, the Federation
       provides $140,000 worth of funding to the Jewish Community
       Center annually. The Jewish Community Center operating
       budget is $1,292,000. The money received from the
       Federation reflects 9% of the Jewish Community Center's
       operating budget."

                                -5-

For fiscal years '89-90, the Federation's operating budget was
$1,686,000. Of that sum, the Center received $142,000. For fiscal
years '90-91, the Federation's operating budget is projected to be
$2,186,354 of which the Center has been allocated $167,000.

The major purpose of the Federation is to raise money for
various charitable beneficiaries. At the present time, there are 39
separate beneficiaries of the Jewish Federation's allocations. The
Center is one of them. Three other groups or agencies, the United
Jewish Appeal, Operation Exodus, and Jewish Family Services (Engres)
receive larger allocations. These allocations range in size from
close to 500,000 to the UJA to $180 dollars to Temple Beth Sholom.
In no sense is the Community Center singled out by the Federation as
the recipient of special treatment or special allocations. A summary
of the Federation's allocations for '89-90 and '90-91 is attached to
this letter for your inspection.

The Jewish Community Center is also the recipient of some 92,000
dollars from the United Way, and, equally is in no sense an
apparent instrumentality of that charitable organization either. The
Jewish Community Center is a beneficiary agency of both these major
charitable organizations, the Jewish Federation of Greater San Jose
and the United Way of Santa Clara County.

4.      "The Federation is a non-profit, tax exempt charitable
organization. It provided airfare and reasonable
subsistence to three council members to allow them to
attend the annual Conference of Mayors held in Israel in
April 1989 and 1990. The trip was valued at $2,100 and
was determined by this office, in consultation with the
staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC),
to be a gift."

The 9th Jerusalem Conference of Mayors was held in April of
1989. Among others, it was attended by the Honorable Joanne
Benjamin, Mayor of Los Gatos at the time, the Honorable Thomas J.
Perillo, Vice Mayor of Los Gatos at the time. This Conference was
also attended by five other municipal or county representatives from
the United States, as well as 24 other mayors and city officials
representing countries from around the world. In fact, the Mayor of
Los Gatos, Joanne Benjamin, was the Chair for the second session at
the Jerusalem Center for Planning in Historic Cities covering the
topic "Revitalization of the City Center: Impacts on Urban Quality of
Life."

The Jewish Federation requested a letter opinion from your
office on the 27 day of October, 1989 to make certain that providing
such trips for these conferences would not generate conflicts of
interest. Your office gave its opinion and approval on December 12,
1989.

-6-

These mayoral conferences began in 1980 as a continuing program
and have been attended by at least 80 mayors of United States cities
or county officers. They have been attended by mayors from small
cities, such as Nutley, New Jersey, to major cities, such as Chicago,
Illinois. They have been attended by mayors from both coasts, from
the south, from the north, and throughout the United States. The
attendance at these conferences by the city council persons
identified in the opinion was by no means specially tailored for
their benefit, nor were they singled out for special considerations.
On the contrary, as the record reflects, they attended these meetings
as part of a continuing program designed to elucidate, to educate,
and to inform municipal operatives on various urban issues.

Copies of the Conference Brochure and a list of the American
participants in the annual conferences are attached to this letter
for your information, as well.

What, then is the Jewish Federation; what is the Jewish
Community Center; what is the relationship between the two?

The Jewish Community Center is a service organization aimed at
meeting the health, recreational, cultural, social and athletic needs
of children, adults, families, the elderly and individuals with
special needs through a variety of programs. Our goal, to the extent
possible, is to run all of our programs on a fee for services break
even basis but because many of our clientel cannot afford to pay the
full fee, the Center offers a wide range of scholarship and
subsidized programs. To make ends meet, the JCC looks to its members
in the form of increased fees, to the Federation and the United Way
in our annual appeal for funds and to the leadership of the JCC for
additional fund raising efforts.

The Mission of the Jewish Federation is to develop leadership,
to raise and allocate funds needed to maintain and strengthen
Jewish identity, Jewish community and Jewish life, and to be an
advocate for the needs and concerns of the Jewish community in the
South Bay, the United States, Israel and the rest of the world.

The relationship between the Jewish Community Center and the
Federation is one of cooperation and friendship as both are 501(c)(3)
charitable organizations dedicated to providing services to both the
Jewish and non-Jewish communities in the Greater San Jose area.

We believe that the advice given to the members of the Town
Council by the Town Attorney regarding the appearance of a conflict
of interest is not supported by the facts. We are very concerned
that the actions taken by the Town Attorney has unfairly cast a
public shadow of doubt over our pending appearance before the Town
Council.

We very much hope that your ruling will remove this shadow which
will allow us to proceed to argue the case for our land use permit on
the merits of the application.

-7-

I would greatly appreciate receiving a copy of your ruling.

Please feel free to call me should you have any questions.

Yours truly,

[illegible signature]

Robert Hudson
JCC President

     Michael Papo, Executive Director,
          Federation of Greater San Jose

line 14 a correction in the following sentence: "At
the present time, the base rent is $.44 per square
foot..." The corrected sentence should read "...the
base rent is $.75 per square foot pursuant to lease
structured increases for an effective of $1.19 per
square foot."

Yours truly,

Robert Hudson

sections.facts:
The Federation is a nonprofit, tax-exempt charitable organization. It provided air
fare and reasonable subsistence to three councilmembers to allow them to attend
the annual Conference of Mayors held in Israel in April of 1989 and 1990. The trip
was valued at $2100 and was determined by this office, in consultation with the staff
of the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC), to be a gift.
The JCC applied to the Town for an architectural and site permit and a conditional
use permit to expand its facilities by constructing a health and fitness center which
has been valued at $3,051,568. (See the attached memorandum from Scott Baker,
Director of Building Services.) The permits were denied by the Planning
Commission and the JCC is now appealing to the Town Council. The hearing on
the appeal was continued from June 18, 1990 to July 16, 1990.
Although the Federation and the JCC are incorporated separately, hold autonomous
meetings of the Board of Directors and have independent operating policies, they
also share four (4) common board members. Additionally, the Federation is housed
at the JCC facility where it pays .75 cents per square foot a month (a rent which is
considered below market) and of a $1.5 million operating budget, the Federation
provides $140,000 worth of funding to the JCC annually. The yearly operating
budget for the JCC is $1,292,000; the money received from the Federation representi
9 percent of that budget.
Enclosed you will find my agenda report to Council, which includes an excerpted
confidential opinion on the subject. It is respectfully requested that you expedite the
issuance of your advice letter since the Town council is scheduled to ad^dress this
matter on July 16, 1990.
TOWN EVALUATION
The- analysis of the applicable statutes and regulations concludes that there is, at a
minimum, the appeararce of a conflict of interest concerning the councilmembers'
participation on the JCC appeal.


Ms. Kathryn Donovan
June 19, 1990
Acceptance of the trip to Israel was not a violation of Proposition 73 (Government
Code Section 85400) which prohibits elected officials from accepting from a single
source a gift or gifts aggregating $1,000 or more in any calendar year because the
money provided by the Federation was solely reimbursement for actual
transportation expenses and subsistence in connection with chairing and participating
in panel discussions on governmental processes.
To avoid the burden of proving that the councilmembers provided consideration of
equal or greater value for the cost of the trip ($2,100) pursuant to Government Code
82028,1recommended to the Councilmembers that the trip be considered a gift and
not income.
Government Code Section 87103(e) provides that an official who receives gifts
aggregating $250 or more must disqualiff himself or herself from pa{icipating in any
decision which would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
source of the gifts. FPPC Regulation 18702.5 provides guidance on determining
whether the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision on a nonprofit organization
which has made gifts will be considered material.
In reviewing how FPPC Regulation 18702.5(d)(2) applied to the Federation and the
JCC, although it was unclear whether or not the health and fitness center would
effect the allocation of funds from the Federation to the JCC, it was clear that the
JCC's assets would increase by over $100,000 dollars since the new building was
valued at $3,051,568.
Furthermore, it is possible the Federation would either incur or avoid additional
expenses or reduce or eliminate existing expenses in the amount of $25,000 by virtue
of its connections with the JCC. Neither the Federation or the JCC have provided
this office with any information that the Federation would not be effected by the
construction of the health and fitness building.
Based on this analysis, I have given conservative advice that the three
councilmembers consider disqualifying themselves, and that the "legally required
participation" process provided by Regulation 18701 be followed. The Council has
requested an advice letter from the FPPC to clariry the councilmembers'
participation.
Again, I would sincerely appreciate your prompt attention to this matter. If you
have further questions, please feel free to contact me. Further information about


Ms. Kathryn Donovan
June L9, L990
the organizations involved may be obtained from Michael Papo, Executive Director,
Jewish Federation of Greater San Jose at (408) 358-3033. You may also wish to
reference FPPC advice letter to Mr. Papo A-39-636 (December 12, 1989).

embedding.first_500_words: FPPC JUN 20 11:27 AM '90 TOWN of LOS GATOS Office of the Town Attorney (408) 354-6880 Federal Express June 19, 1990 Ms. Kathryn E. Donovan General Counsel, Legal Division Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 Re: Request for Advice on behalf of Councilmembers Thomas J. Ferrito, Brent N. Ventura and Joanne Benjamin. Dear Ms. Donovan: I have been authorized by Thomas J. Ferrito, Brent N. Ventura and Joanne Benjamin, Councilmembers of the Town of Los Gatos, to seek on their behalf this request for formal written advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b). Councilmember Thomas J. Ferrito's mailing address is 9 Simons Way, Los Gatos, CA 95030. Councilmember Brent Ventura's mailing address is 131 Broadway, Los Gatos, CA 95030. Councilmember Joanne Benjamin's mailing address is 109 Worcester, Los Gatos, CA 95030. This request seeks guidance on the obligations under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1976 for Councilmembers Ferrito, Ventura and Benjamin. More specifically, we request your opinion regarding whether the acceptance of travel and subsistence expenses provided by the Jewish Federation of Greater San Jose (Federation) in connection with the annual Conference of Mayors held in Israel requires disqualification from participation in a land use decision which would have a material financial effect on the Jewish Community Center (JCC). The difficulty in the analysis of this situation stems from the fact that the CIVIC CENTER • 110 EAST MAIN STREET • P.O. BOX 949 • LOS GATOS, CALIFORNIA 95031 Ms. Kathryn Donovan June 19, 1990 Page 2 Federation has a significant relationship with the JCC, such that the JCC may appear to be an "instrumentality" of the Federation. STATEMENT OF FACTS The Federation is a nonprofit, tax-exempt charitable organization. It provided air fare and reasonable subsistence to three councilmembers to allow them to attend the annual Conference of Mayors held in Israel in April of 1989 and 1990. The trip was valued at $2100 and was determined by this office, in consultation with the staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC), to be a gift. The JCC applied to the Town for an architectural and site permit and a conditional use permit to expand its facilities by constructing a health and fitness center which has been valued at $3,051,568. (See the attached memorandum from Scott Baker, Director of Building Services.) The permits were denied by the Planning Commission and the JCC is now appealing to the Town Council. The hearing on the appeal was continued from June 18, 1990 to July 16, 1990. Although the Federation and the JCC are incorporated separately, hold autonomous meetings of the Board of Directors and have independent operating policies, they also share four (4) common board members. Additionally, the Federation is housed at the JCC facility where it pays .75 cents per square foot a month (a rent which is considered below market) and of a $1.5 million operating budget, the Federation provides $140,000 worth of funding to the JCC
